title: Trauma and the Body's Roots: Grounding, Ancestry, and Somatic Healing
excerpt: Explore how trauma lives in the body, ancestral patterns influence healing, and grounding restores nervous system balance.
body:
Introduction: Why Trauma Lives in the Body

Trauma isn’t just a story lodged in your mind; it’s a lived experience embedded deeply in your body’s nervous system. As Bessel van der Kolk famously wrote in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma rewires our physiology, leaving us stuck in a state of hyperarousal, shutdown, or dissociation long after the triggering event has passed. This physiological imprint makes healing complex and often elusive, especially when traditional talk therapy falls short.

Understanding trauma’s roots requires looking beyond the individual and into the intergenerational transmission of trauma—from our ancestors’ experiences to how our bodies have learned to survive. This article explores the science-backed role of grounding, ancestry, and somatic healing in reclaiming nervous system regulation and restoring wholeness.

The Science of Trauma in the Nervous System

Peter Levine’s work in Somatic Experiencing highlights how trauma disrupts the natural rhythms of the autonomic nervous system (ANS). Instead of completing a natural response to danger—like fight, flight, or freeze—the body gets stuck, holding residual energy in muscles, fascia, and neural pathways. This incomplete discharge creates chronic tension, pain, and dysregulation.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory further clarifies how trauma affects the vagus nerve, our main parasympathetic pathway. Trauma often traps individuals in defensive states: either hypervigilant (sympathetic activation) or immobilized (dorsal vagal shutdown). Both states disconnect us from safety cues and social engagement, making it difficult to feel grounded or present.

Intergenerational Trauma: The Body Remembers Beyond Our Life

Trauma doesn’t stop at our own experiences. Research in epigenetics and epigenetic inheritance shows that trauma can alter gene expression, affecting offspring’s stress responses and vulnerability to mental health challenges. Gabor Maté emphasizes how family dynamics, cultural history, and ancestral wounds shape our nervous systems. We inherit not just genetics but also the emotional imprints and survival patterns of those who came before us.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy integrates this understanding, encouraging awareness of how ancestral trauma manifests in posture, movement habits, and somatic patterns. Healing is not just about processing personal events but also acknowledging and releasing these embedded familial legacies.

Grounding: The Foundation of Somatic Healing

Grounding is often misunderstood as merely “being present” or “feeling your feet.” In somatic healing, grounding refers to reconnecting with the body’s sensations and the environment to restore nervous system balance. It’s a tangible tool for shifting out of dysregulated states and into safety.

Deb Dana, a leading Polyvagal clinician, offers practical grounding techniques that engage the ventral vagal complex—our social engagement system. These include slow, intentional breathing, feeling the support of the ground beneath you, and using tactile sensations (like holding a stone or textured object) to signal safety to the brain.

Grounding is essential because it interrupts the trauma loop. By anchoring in the body and present moment, we enable the nervous system to complete its defensive responses and return to homeostasis.

Somatic Healing Modalities: Beyond Talk Therapy

Traditional talk therapy often addresses trauma cognitively, which can be insufficient when the body holds the unresolved energy. Somatic therapies, pioneered by Francine Shapiro, Peter Levine, and Pat Ogden, incorporate body awareness and movement to access and release trauma stored below conscious awareness.

  • Somatic Experiencing (Peter Levine): Focuses on tracking bodily sensations to renegotiate trauma responses.
  • Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden): Combines talk therapy with movement and posture to process trauma.
  • Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) (Francine Shapiro): Uses bilateral stimulation to reprocess traumatic memories stored in the body.

These approaches emphasize the body’s innate capacity to heal, encouraging gentle, incremental shifts rather than forcing cognitive insight alone.

Integrating Ancestral Awareness in Healing

Recognizing ancestral trauma invites a deeper level of compassion and context for self-healing. Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model can be extended to include ancestral parts—internalized voices or somatic patterns that represent family or cultural history.

By bringing these ancestral influences into conscious awareness—through journaling, ritual, or somatic inquiry—we can begin to disentangle personal trauma from inherited pain. This process reduces shame and isolation, fostering a sense of belonging to a lineage rather than a solitary suffering.

Practical Grounding Exercises to Begin Somatic Healing

  • Body Scan: Slowly move your attention through your body, noting sensations without judgment. This cultivates interoception, a key skill identified by Dan Siegel to enhance self-regulation.
  • Feet on the Ground: Sit with feet flat on the floor. Press and release the soles, feeling the support beneath you. This tactile feedback helps shift vagal tone toward safety.
  • Breath Awareness: Practice slow, diaphragmatic breathing, extending the exhale. This activates the parasympathetic nervous system.
  • Movement Exploration: Gentle swaying, rocking, or stretching can discharge trapped energy and re-establish fluid movement patterns.

Conclusion: Reclaiming the Body’s Wisdom

Trauma’s roots run deep—into our bodies and across generations. Healing requires a grounded, embodied approach that honors this complexity. By integrating somatic healing practices, acknowledging ancestral influences, and cultivating nervous system regulation, we can move from survival to vitality.

“The body keeps the score, but it also holds the power to heal—when we learn to listen and respond with presence and care.” — Adapted from Bessel van der Kolk

For those who have felt stuck despite therapy, somatic healing offers a direct path to reconnect with the body’s innate wisdom and restore a sense of safety, belonging, and wholeness.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. A. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Publications.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the body differently than the mind?
faqs.a: Trauma imprints on the nervous system, creating physiological states of hyperarousal or shutdown that persist beyond conscious memory. This explains why symptoms often appear as physical tension, pain, or dysregulation rather than just mental distress.

faqs.q: What is grounding and why is it important for healing trauma?
faqs.a: Grounding reconnects you with your body and present environment, helping regulate the nervous system. It interrupts trauma’s freeze or hypervigilance cycles, allowing for safety and nervous system balance to return.

faqs.q: How can ancestral trauma influence my own healing journey?
faqs.a: Ancestral trauma can shape your nervous system through epigenetic changes and learned survival patterns. Recognizing these influences can provide context and compassion, helping you untangle inherited pain from your personal experience.

faqs.q: Are somatic therapies effective for those stuck in traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapies engage the body directly, accessing trauma held below conscious awareness. This often unlocks stuck energy and facilitates healing when talk therapy alone hasn’t been enough.
